segments.s363-g49: درین ماه = در + این fused into درین, meaning 'in this month' (prepositional phrase). ار is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a poetic/colloquial temporal particle 'now/at this moment' placed before the verb. خواهد is third-person singular 'wills/will' (future/optative); the verb appears before its subject خدای by poetic inversion, so the clause reads 'if now God wills'.
segments.s363-g50: بپوشم is first-person subjunctive/optative with the b- prefix ('may I put on' / 'I will put on') used after the conditional. به رزم = 'for battle' (به + noun: purpose/occasion). آهنینه is an adjective 'iron(-like)/steely' modifying قبای 'robe' (poetic adjective + noun order). Together the phrase means 'I will put on a steely robe for battle.'
segments.s363-g51: به توران زمین = prepositional phrase 'to/into the land of Turan' (به + place + زمین). اندر is another prepositional particle 'in/within' — poetic doubling of locative marking. آرم is a first-person verb form 'I will station / I will set' (poetic/archaic order) and سپاه is its object 'army' (verb before object is common in Ferdowsi's diction).
segments.s363-g52: کنم is the first-person verb 'I will do / I will make' used here as a causative/resultive auxiliary. کشور گرگساران is the direct object 'the country/realm of the wolfish ones' (گرگساران = 'wolfish people' or 'wolves' used metaphorically). تباه is the predicate/adjectival result 'ruined/laid waste' — کنَم ... تباه = 'I will make ... ruined' = 'I will destroy ...'.
segments.s363-g1: همان چون functions as a temporal conjunction 'just when / as soon as'. بگفت is the older, prefixed past-tense form (ب‑ + گفت) meaning 'he said'; in classical Persian the verb often appears before its subject. این سخن is the direct object 'these words / this speech', so the whole chunk reads 'just when the king said these words'.
segments.s363-g2: This is an appositive/name-list: زریر (a proper name) followed by سپهدار, a title identifying him ('the commander'), then و اسفندیار ('and Esfandiyar'). The title follows the name (typical in Persian epics) and the whole phrase names the actors who act after the king spoke.
segments.s363-g3: کشیدند is past plural 'they drew' (here 'drew/unsheathed' a weapon; classical epic uses کشیدن for drawing a sword). شمشیر is the direct object 'swords'. و گفتند joins the action and introduces their speech ('and they said'). اگر begins the conditional clause 'if'.
segments.s363-g4: کسی باشد uses کسی ('someone/anyone') plus باشد, the present/subjunctive form 'there be' used in classical Persian to express existence within a conditional ('if there is anyone'). اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world'. سربسر here intensifies the scope — 'anywhere / at all / throughout' the world.
segments.s363-g5: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). نپسندد is the negative present/subjunctive (or general present) of پسندیدن 'to approve/like' and here means 'does not approve' or 'would not approve'. او را is the object 'him' with the definite object marker را. به دین‌آوری uses به as 'with/regarding/for' and دین‌آوری means 'piety / religious conduct' — together 'who does not approve him for his piety' (i.e. does not accept him because of matters of religion).
segments.s363-g6: سر اندر نیارد is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic expression: سر 'head' + اندر 'into' + نیارد, here an archaic/palatalized form equivalent to نیاورد/نیاوردَ (negative present/subjunctive of آوردن) meaning 'does not put/bring' — idiomatically 'does not place his head into' = 'does not submit/bow'. به فرمانبری is the prepositional phrase 'into obedience / to obedience'. The whole unit says he will not submit his head in obedience.
segments.s363-g7: نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form of آمدن in classical style ('will not come / does not come'). بدرگاه is the compound prepositional noun 'into the (royal) court / into the presence (of the king)'. فرخنده شاه is 'the auspicious/fortunate king' with فرخنده qualifying شاه; word order puts the court-term before the king's epithet, a poetic arrangement meaning 'into the auspicious king's court/presence'.
segments.s363-g8: نبندد is negative present/subjunctive of بستن 'to close / to bar' and here means 'will not bar / will not interpose'. میان 'between' + پیش 'before / in the presence of' form a doubled spatial phrase 'between and before' (poetic redundancy) and رخشنده گاه is 'the radiant/shining place/hall' (گاه used as 'place/occasion' in compounds). Together it says he does not place himself between and before the shining court — i.e. he does not appear/stand before the radiant hall.
segments.s363-g25: نوشتند is the 3rd-person plural past tense ‘they wrote’. نامه is the direct object ‘letter’ following the verb (typical of older Persian flexibility). به + ارجاسپ marks the addressee ‘to Arjasp’ (به = ‘to/for’). زشت is an epithet after the name and modifies ارجاسپ (‘Arjasp the vile/ugly’).
segments.s363-g26: هم is an adverbial particle here (ʻalso/tooʼ) adding that additional information follows. اندر is the locative/prepositional particle ʻin/withinʼ; خور is uncertain/archaic in this line (literally ʻsunʼ in the dictionary entry, but here it functions as a spatial/interior noun — read as ʻinterior/insideʼ in context); آن is the demonstrative ʻthat/itsʼ. کجا normally means ʻwhereʼ and introduces the following clause; او is the 3rd-person pronoun ʻhe/itʼ; نوشت is the 3rd-person singular past ʻwroteʼ. Taken together the phrase says essentially ʻand also in its interior — where he wroteʼ; the precise referent of خور is idiomatic/poetic and therefore ambiguous, so the clause is translated literally as a locative comment.
segments.s363-g27: زریر is the proper name; سپهبد is the title ‘spahbed’ (general) following the name as an appositive. گرفتش is the past form ʻhe seized/ took itʼ — the clitic ش marks the direct object ʻitʼ. به دست is a purposive/locative phrase ʻin (his) handʼ (به + noun = ʻin/on/withʼ).
segments.s363-g28: چنان هم = ʻthus/so, tooʼ (چنان = ʻsuch/soʼ, هم as an emphatic/continuative ʻalso/soʼ). گشاده is an adjective ʻopenʼ describing the state of the letter/packet. ببردش is an older/stylistic verbal form meaning ʻhe carried it away/ he bore itʼ (ب‑ prefix + برد + object clitic ش). نبست is the negative past of بستن ʻto close/tie/sealʼ — here ʻhe did not close/seal itʼ. The sequence describes that he carried it while it remained open.
segments.s363-g29: سوی شاه برد: سوی = ʻtoward/toʼ, شاه = ʻthe kingʼ; برد here is a past-tense verb ʻbrought/took (it)ʼ (note: the lemma senses provided did not include this verbal sense, so the sense index is set to -1). و = ʻandʼ; برو is an imperative/archaic/infixal infinitive-like form meaning ʻhe went/goingʼ or ʻhe proceededʼ; بر here functions as ʻbefore/in the presence ofʼ; بخواند is 3rd-person singular past/subjunctive of خواندن meaning ʻread/reciteʼ or ʻcause to be readʼ. Together: ʻHe brought it to the king and (there) went and read it aloud before him.ʼ
segments.s363-g30: جهانجوی is an epithet literally ʻworld-seekingʼ (an adjective/noun describing ambition) modifying the proper name گشتاسپ (Gushtasp). خیره is an adjective here meaning ʻastonished/aghastʼ or ʻstaringʼ; بماند is the past/stative ʻremained/was leftʼ. The phrase describes Gushtasp remaining astonished on hearing the contents.
segments.s363-g31: ز is the poetic preposition ʻfrom/ofʼ that introduces the agent/source. دانا is an adjective ʻwise/learnedʼ placed before سپهبد (a title) to qualify it: ʻthe wise spahbedʼ. زریر is the proper name, and سوار is an appositive/adjectival noun ʻrider/horsemanʼ attached to the name. The whole is a terse, appositive-style listing common in epic prose.
segments.s363-g32: ز جاماسپ = ʻfrom Jamaspʼ (ز = ʻfromʼ, جاماسپ proper name). و = ʻandʼ. ز فرخ اسفندیار = ʻfrom the fortunate (فرخ) Esfandiyarʼ; فرخ is an epithet ʻfortunate/blessedʼ placed before the name اسفندیار. This continues the same catalogue-of-senders construction.
segments.s363-g41: چنین تا بدانستی is a purpose phrase: چنین 'thus/so' + تا introducing a purpose/result clause ('so that/in order that'), and بدانستی here functions as a jussive/subjunctive-like 'may you know' (the form is homographic with a past but used to mark the intended result). آن گرگسار is a demonstrative + noun: آن 'that/the' points to a specific place, گرگسار 'wolf-infested place' (a descriptive noun) follows normal noun phrase order.
segments.s363-g42: که introduces a relative clause modifying گرگسار ('which/that'), and the clause is head-final: گردن 'neck' is the body-part noun, followed by نیازد (verb) and then ابا which here carries the sense 'to be reluctant/refuse' so the sequence expresses 'refuses to supplicate/bow (its) neck' to شهریار (the king). The overall order is relative particle + [object/part + verb + modal/attitudinal word + indirect object].
segments.s363-g43: بینداخت نامه is verb + object (he cast/threw the letter). بگفتا is the past narrative 'he said' introducing reported speech, and روید functions as the reported imperative/command ('rise/go/advance') — the exact form is ambiguous (appearance vs. imperative in the lexicon), but syntactically it is the speech act following 'he said'.
segments.s363-g44: مرین را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker on the proper name Merin (مرین treated as an object). سوی is the preposition 'toward', and ترک جادو is a noun phrase 'Turk of magic' (i.e. a sorcerer/Turk-magician) functioning as the goal; برید (from بردن) here means 'take/carry' (command or perfective action depending on context) and completes the imperative sense of the clause.
segments.s363-g45: بگویید here introduces reported speech (imperative/reporting 'they said' / 'tell them'); هوشت is the noun 'your hoosh/awareness' (hoosh with the enclitic -t 'your') used as the theme; فراز آمدست is a predicate phrase where فراز 'on high/above' combines with آمدست 'has come/has risen' to mean 'has risen on high' — the verbal form آمدست is perfective/resultative.
segments.s363-g46: This is built from two prepositional phrases (به خون 'to/for your blood' and به خاکت 'to/for your dust') followed by the nominal predicate نیاز آمدست 'a need/plea has come'. Here نیاز is used as an existential/event noun 'need/supplication' and آمدست is the resultative 'has come', so literally 'a need has come upon your blood and upon your dust.'
segments.s363-g47: زده باد is an optative formula: زده (past participle 'struck') + باد (optative particle 'may') gives 'may [it] be struck'. گردنت خسته میان places the possessed noun گردنِ-ت 'your neck' followed by the adjective خسته 'weary' and the locative میان 'in the midst/among', specifying where the striking should occur; word order puts the possessor-marked noun before modifiers.
segments.s363-g48: به خاک اندرون is a locative prepositional phrase 'into/to the inner earth' and ریخته is a past-participle adjective 'scattered/poured' that modifies or predicates about استخوان 'bones/remains'. The sequence is typical: locative phrase + participle + noun, yielding 'bones having been scattered into the inner earth.'
segments.s363-g33: ببست and نوشت are past tense verbs: 'he sealed/closed' (ببست) and 'he wrote' (نوشت). اندرو is a locative adverb meaning 'in/within it' placed after the verb (typical older/poetic order). نام خویش ش is the object 'his own name' (خویش split into خوی + ش here), with ش as the possessive 'his'.
segments.s363-g34: فرستادگان را is the definite/object phrase 'the messengers' marked by را; همه quantifies them ('all'). خواند here means 'he called/summoned' (a past action), and پیش is a locative/adverbial meaning 'before him / forward', placed after the verb.
segments.s363-g35: بگیرید is an imperative/plural 'seize/take (you all)!' used as a command. گفت is the report verb 'he said' introducing the speech; این points to what was said ('this' / 'these words'). زی here is the preposition 'from/of' and او is the third‑person pronoun 'him/it'; برید functions as past 'took' (classical usage of برد/برید to mean 'took away' here), so the phrase reports: 'Take them,' he said, 'and take him from there.'
segments.s363-g36: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see!'; زین is a noun 'this saddle / harness' and is its direct object of the command. سپس 'then/thereupon' links to what follows. راه را is the object 'the way/road' (را marks the definite object). نسپرید is the negative/past form of سپردن ('did not entrust / did not hand over'), so the clause says he did not entrust/commit the route afterward.
segments.s363-g37: که‌گر is a conditional particle 'if'; نیستی is the second‑person present/predicative 'you are not'. اندر استا is an archaic locative + verb phrase meaning 'stand inside / remain within' (اندر = in/within, استا = stand). و زند links and introduces زند used as a verb/noun root meaning 'keep alive' or 'preserve life' (classical terse phrasing).
segments.s363-g38: فرستاده را is the direct object 'the messenger' (را marks definiteness/object). زینهار is an admonitive or hortative 'take heed / protect' used here as an injunction. از گزند is a prepositional phrase 'from harm' (از = from; گزند = harm), so the whole clause means 'guard the messenger from harm' or 'beware of harm to the messenger.'
segments.s363-g39: ازین ('از' + 'ین/این') is 'from this' (the older orthography ین = این); خواب means 'sleep/dream' and is the source from which the action takes place. بیدارتان is the object+possessive 'I awakened you (pl.)' (بیدار = awake, -تان = your/you plural), and کردمی is the first‑person past 'I did' (archaic past form). Together: 'I awoke you from this sleep.'
segments.s363-g40: همان is a deictic 'that same' or 'the very one' modifying زنده 'alive / the living one'. بر is the prepositional particle 'to/before/upon' used in older prose to mark direction or placement toward someone. دارتان is a possessive phrase with -تان 'your' attached to a noun dār (here used poetically for 'presence/stand/position' or 'place before you'); کردمی is again the past 'I did'. The clause reads 'I likewise brought that living one before you' (lit. 'made that same living [one] upon/ before your place').
segments.s363-g9: نگیرد is the negative-present/jussive form of گرفتن (here 'take/seize') with the prefixed ن- (let not / does not). ازو is the prepositional clitic 'from him'. راه و دین بهی is a coordinated noun phrase ('way and true/good religion'), where بهی is an adjective after the noun (typical in classical Persian) modifying دین. The whole segment says roughly 'let him not take from him the way and true faith.'
segments.s363-g10: مرین is a plural imperative/jussive ('may they die' / 'die!') formed with -ین; it functions as an interjection-like command. دین به is 'دینِ به' = 'good/upright religion' (noun + postpositive adjective), را is the (enclitic) object/emphasis marker attached after the phrase, نباشد is the negative subjunctive/jussive of بودن ('there is not' / 'may there not be'), and رهی (poetic/archaic form related to راه) names the 'way/path'. Together: 'May they die — there is no way for (their) good religion.'
segments.s363-g11: به + شمشیر marks instrument ('by/with the sword'); جان is the direct object 'life/soul'; از تنش is a prepositional phrase 'from his body' (تن + possessive ش); بر کنیم is the compound verb (بر- as verbal prefix + کنیم) in the 1st person plural subjunctive/hortative — used here as a future/commanding 'we will take/remove'. Word order — instrument + object + source + verb — is typical in elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s363-g12: سرش را is the definite object ('his head' + object marker را). به دار is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the gallows' (دار here = gallows). برین is a demonstrative/poetic form (variant of 'آن/این') pointing to that gallows; بر کنیم repeats the verbal compound (بر- + کنیم) meaning 'we will place/put up'. The repetition and order are rhetorical/poetic: object → goal → demonstrative → verb.
segments.s363-g13: سپهدار ایران is a title + placename in apposition: 'the commander of Iran'. که introduces a relative clause 'whose/whose name', and نامش is 'his name' (نام + enclitic ش). زریر is the proper name filling that slot. Word order: title/place → relative → possessor + name.
segments.s363-g14: نبرده is the perfect/past form of بردن with the negative prefix ن- in the supplied lexicon ('has not taken' / 'did not take'), but in classical/poetic usage this construction often describes possession or display of a quality (here bravery) rather than a literal negation — renderable as 'bore/possessed'. دلیری is the abstract noun 'bravery'; چو is a comparative/simile 'like/as'; درنده شیر is a genitive-like compound 'predatory/ferocious lion'. Together: 'he (has) bravery like a ferocious lion.'
segments.s363-g15: به شاه جهان is the prepositional phrase 'to the King of the World' (به + title). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' (here used as a simple past reporting speech). آزاده‌وار is a manner adverb 'nobly/magnanimously' modifying گفت. Order is typical: indirect object (to-phrase) + verb + adverbial manner.
segments.s363-g16: که introduces the content clause of speech ('that/so that'). دستور باشد is a jussive/subjunctive clause 'let there be an order/let an order be' (باشد = subjunctive of بودن). مرا is the object pronoun 'me' plus the enclitic را (here split into two tokens in the data: م + را) meaning 'for me / concerning me' (object marking). شهریار is vocative/appellative 'O sovereign / king' addressing the monarch. The clause reads as a requested order/appointment for the speaker.
segments.s363-g17: که introduces a content clause ('that ...') describing what is pleasing. پاسخ کنم is a subordinated verbal clause: پاسخ (noun 'answer') with کنم (1st‑person-form of کردن used here as the verb 'to answer') yielding 'that I should answer' or 'to answer'. جادو precedes ارجاسپ را in poetical word order; جادو names the thing to be answered ('magic'), and ارجاسپ را (ارجاسپ + object marker را) specifies whose magic it is ('Arjasp's magic') and marks the definite object/target of the answering.
segments.s363-g18: پسند آمد is an impersonal construction 'it came pleasing' = 'it pleased'; این points back to the preceding clause (the thing that pleased). شاه گشتاسپ را is the experiencer marked by را (literary/object/experiencer marking): literal order 'pleasing came this to King Gushtasp' = 'this pleased King Gushtasp.'
segments.s363-g19: بدو گفت is inverted word order common in poetry: 'to him said' = 'he said to him'. برخیز is the imperative 'rise!' and و links to the next command. پاسخ کنش is an imperative clause: پاسخ 'an answer' + کنش, an archaic/poetic imperative/subjunctive form of کردن here meaning 'do (give) it' = 'answer!' (the enclitic -ش is a classical form).
segments.s363-g20: This is an elliptic sequence of commands: نکال 'vengeance/punishment' named as the action to be taken, تگینان (plural of Tegin) designates the target, خلخ 'anklet' names an object associated with them, and کنش again is the poetic imperative 'do it' — together conveying 'exact vengeance upon the Teginan; let their anklets clink' (the exact nuanced action is left elliptical as is common in epic diction).
segments.s363-g21: A simple nominal coordination: زریر modified by the adjective گرانمایه ('precious/esteemed') joined by و to اسفندیار. Word order places the epithet immediately after the name, a common poetic placement.
segments.s363-g22: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. جاماسپ is the agent/name. دستور ناباک‌دار is a noun phrase 'a[n] order/command that is ناباک‌دار' (ناباک‌دار = morally impure/dishonorable); the clause is largely nominal/elliptical (no overt verb), typical in epic lines where this temporal phrase links forward to the following action.
segments.s363-g23: ز پیشش is a prepositional phrase 'from before him / from his presence' placed before the verb for emphasis. برفتند is the third-person plural past 'they departed'. هر سه به هم ('each/ all three together') emphasizes that the three left in company.
segments.s363-g24: شده is a perfect/participial 'have become' describing their state. سر پر از کین literally 'head(s) full of hatred' uses سر metonymically for mind/thought and پر از 'full of' followed by کین. دلها دژم is verbless predication typical of Persian poetry: 'their hearts [were] sullen/grim.'

sentences.s363-s25: If now God wills it in this month, I will don a steely battle-robe.
sentences.s363-s26: I will station an army in the land of Turan; I will lay waste the land of the wolfish ones.
sentences.s363-s1: Just as the king spoke these words, Zarihr the commander and Esfandiyar…
sentences.s363-s2: They drew their swords and said, 'If there is anyone anywhere in the world…'
sentences.s363-s3: who would not approve of him for his piety and would not bow his head in obedience,
sentences.s363-s4: he will not come into the auspicious king's presence, nor will he stand before the radiant court.
sentences.s363-s13: They wrote a letter to Arjasp the vile; and (also) in its interior — where he had written.
sentences.s363-s14: Zarir the spahbed took it in his hand; he carried it thus open and did not close (seal) it.
sentences.s363-s15: He took it to the king and went up to read it before him; Gushtasp the world-seeker stood amazed.
sentences.s363-s16: From the wise spahbed Zarir the rider, from Jamasp, and from the fortunate Esfandiyar.
sentences.s363-s21: So that you may know that wolf-infested place which refuses to bow its neck to the king.
sentences.s363-s22: He threw the letter and said, 'Go — take Merin toward the Turk of sorcery.'
sentences.s363-s23: They said, 'Your awareness has risen high; a need has come upon your blood and upon your dust.'
sentences.s363-s24: May your weary neck be struck in the midst; your bones lie scattered within the earth.
sentences.s363-s17: He sealed it and wrote therein his own name; then he summoned all the messengers before him.
sentences.s363-s18: “Seize them,” he said; “and take him from there.” Then: “Look at the saddle,” and afterward he did not entrust the road.
sentences.s363-s19: If you are not there, stand inside and keep him alive; guard the messenger from harm.
sentences.s363-s20: From this sleep I awakened you; likewise I brought that living man before you.
sentences.s363-s5: Let him take from him neither the way nor the true faith; may they die — there is no path for their religion.
sentences.s363-s6: With the sword we will take his life from his body; we will put his head upon that gallows.
sentences.s363-s7: The spahbed (commander) of Iran, whose name was Zarir, bore a courage like a ferocious lion.
sentences.s363-s8: He said to the King of the World nobly: 'Let there be an appointment/order for me, O sovereign.'
sentences.s363-s9: That I should answer Arjasp's magic pleased this King Gushtasp.
sentences.s363-s10: He said to him, 'Rise and answer him; wreak vengeance on the Teginan — let their anklets jingle.'
sentences.s363-s11: Zarir the esteemed and Esfandiyar — when Jamasp issued the dishonorable order.
sentences.s363-s12: All three departed from his presence together; their heads became full of hatred and their hearts were sullen.
